Scoliosis Rehab Exam Guide

Offline interactive HTML
Overview Core concepts Classification Exam assessment Radiology Treatment algorithm Practical prescriptions Postoperative rehab 30 minute OSCE sequence Interactive tools Quiz hub Sources
How to use:
Read the algorithms first, then use the tables for prescription details. Use the interactive tools for quick treatment reasoning and post-op phase recall. Source tags are shown as U1, SRS, etc.

Structured scoliosis rehabilitation guide for PM&R board examination

This guide converts the uploaded scoliosis notes and postoperative scoliosis rehabilitation review into a practical examination resource. It emphasizes clinical reasoning, physical examination sequence, radiological interpretation, conservative management, orthotic reasoning, postoperative protection, and exercise prescription.

Diagnosis and Cobb angle Physical exam checklist Conservative and brace algorithm Postoperative phase prescription Night mode Quiz and decision tools
Exam framing: scoliosis is not only a lateral curve. For viva answers, start with the three dimensional deformity: coronal lateral curvature, axial rotation, and altered sagittal profile. Then connect the deformity to growth potential, progression risk, respiratory function, pain, body image, function, and rehabilitation goals. U1 U2 SRS

Core concepts to memorize

Definition

Scoliosis is a three dimensional structural spinal deformity involving lateral curvature in the coronal plane, axial rotation in the transverse plane, and disturbance of normal kyphosis or lordosis in the sagittal plane. The practical diagnostic threshold is a Cobb angle of at least 10 degrees on standing radiograph. U1 U2 SRS

Why growth matters

Progression risk is highest during rapid growth. Pubertal growth acceleration, low skeletal maturity, larger Cobb angle, female sex, and residual spinal growth increase concern for progression. The Duval Beaupère concept links curve acceleration to growth velocity. U1 U2 SOSORT

Rehabilitation objectives

The conservative rehabilitation goals are to prevent or slow curve progression, support respiratory function, prevent or treat spinal pain syndromes, improve postural appearance, and maintain participation. In the post-op setting, the central goal is early functional recovery without compromising fusion and instrumentation integrity. U1 U2

Pathomechanics in one chain

Initial imbalanceFunctional or structural asymmetry, growth vulnerability, genetic and environmental interaction.
Concavity contractureShortening of capsule, ligaments, intervertebral tissues, and muscles on the concave side.
RotationVertebral body tends to rotate toward the convexity, rib cage follows the spinal rotation.
WedgingAsymmetric loading changes disc and vertebral growth, with relative shortening on concavity.
Clinical signsRib hump, scapular asymmetry, pelvic obliquity, cosmetic concern, possible restrictive mechanics.

Spine stability model

SubsystemComponentsRehab meaning
PassiveVertebrae, discs, facet joints, spinal ligamentsDefines structural limits and stiffness. In post-op patients, fusion and instrumentation dominate load protection.
ActiveMultifidus, transversus abdominis, internal oblique, paraspinals, pelvic floor, hip and trunk musclesThe most trainable stabilizing system. Core work should teach control, not only isolated strength.
Neural controlSensorimotor integration, postural response, proprioceptionNeeded for corrected posture, balance, gait, return to sport, and ADL carryover.

Exam phrase: spinal stability fails when passive, active, or neural subsystems cannot compensate for one another. U2

Classification and clinical implications

Functional versus structural scoliosis

FeatureFunctional or nonstructuralStructural
CauseSecondary to pain, muscle spasm, leg length discrepancy, disc herniation, spondylolisthesis, hip pathology.Idiopathic, congenital, neuromuscular, syndromic, connective tissue, neurofibromatosis, spinal malformation.
RotationTypically absent or minimal.Axial rotation is present and produces rib hump or lumbar prominence.
FlexibilityCorrects with lateral bending or correction of the underlying driver.Does not fully correct with bending. Structural curve may remain on bending film.
Rehab focusTreat the driver: pain, spasm, LLD, hip or disc pathology.Classify curve, assess growth, prescribe PSSE or brace, refer for surgery when indicated.

Exam key: Adam forward bend test with rib hump suggests structural scoliosis. U1 U2

AIS and other etiologies

GroupExamplesWhat to ask or check
IdiopathicInfantile, juvenile, adolescent idiopathic scoliosis.Age of onset, growth velocity, menarche, Tanner stage, Risser grade, family history.
NeuromuscularCerebral palsy, spinal muscular atrophy, muscular dystrophy, spinal cord lesion.Tone, weakness, sitting balance, pelvic obliquity, respiratory function, nutritional status.
CongenitalHemivertebra, wedge vertebra, segmentation failure, rib synostosis.Associated renal, cardiac, or spinal anomalies when clinically suspected.
Syndromic or connective tissueMarfan syndrome, Ehlers Danlos syndrome, neurofibromatosis.Hypermobility, skin signs, café-au-lait spots, ocular or cardiovascular history.
Lenke classification: what to remember for exam

The uploaded review includes Lenke classification as a comprehensive AIS surgical planning system using curve type, lumbar modifier, and sagittal thoracic modifier. For a PM&R oral exam, you do not need to reproduce every surgical subtype unless asked. You should be able to say that it helps define structural major and minor curves, evaluate coronal and sagittal balance, and guide fusion level selection and postoperative rehabilitation implications. U2

PartClinical questionRehab relevance
Curve typeWhich thoracic, thoracolumbar, or lumbar curves are structural?Predicts movement limitations, muscle imbalance, and fusion level.
Lumbar modifierWhere is the lumbar curve relative to CSVL?Connects to pelvic compensation, gait symmetry, and trunk balance.
Sagittal modifierThoracic kyphosis: hypokyphosis, normal, or hyperkyphosis?Influences posture, breathing mechanics, and exercise caution.

History and physical examination guide

History algorithm

OpeningAge, main concern, cosmetic concern, pain, fatigue, dyspnea, activity limitation.
ProgressionWhen first noticed, curve progression, brace history, prior radiographs, previous treatment.
GrowthMenarche, Tanner signs, growth spurt, height change, skeletal maturity if known.
Etiology screenFamily history, trauma, infection, congenital disorder, neuromuscular symptoms, connective tissue signs.
Red flagsNeurological deficit, bowel or bladder disturbance, severe atypical pain, night pain, fever, weight loss.
FunctionSchool, sport, ADL, body image, psychosocial effect, respiratory limitation.

Physical examination table

Region or domainWhat to checkHow to checkClinical reasoning
General observationBody habitus, syndromic signs, skin lesions, Marfan habitus, café-au-lait spots.Inspect with patient appropriately exposed. Respect privacy and comfort.Identifies non-idiopathic causes and guides referral or imaging.
Growth and anthropometryHeight, weight, arm span, ratio of arm span to height.Measure standing height and fingertip-to-fingertip arm span at 90 degree shoulder abduction.Growth potential affects progression risk. Notes cite normal arm span to height ratio around 1.03 to 1.06. U1
Standing posterior alignmentHead over pelvis, shoulder height, scapular prominence, waist asymmetry, trunk shift.Inspect from posterior and lateral views with feet parallel.Gives quick estimate of coronal and sagittal imbalance.
Plumb lineSpinal alignment from C7 to gluteal cleft or sacrum.Drop line from C7 spinous process, observe deviation relative to midline.Quantifies trunk shift and balance. U1 U2
Shoulder heightSide-to-side shoulder asymmetry.Observe or use scoliometer/level. Notes describe placing the device horizontally and comparing vertical distance.Convex side often appears higher, depending on curve location.
ScapulaScapular winging or prominence, especially convex side.Inspect posteriorly in standing and forward bend.Reflects rotational deformity and thoracic asymmetry.
Rib cageRib hump and rib valley.Adam forward bend test. Small humps are clearer with trunk flexion near 90 degrees.Posterior rib prominence on convex side occurs because vertebral rotation pushes ribs posteriorly. Rib valley is on concave side. U1
PelvisPelvic obliquity, iliac crest height, waist triangle asymmetry.Inspect iliac crests. Use a horizontal board or block method to estimate vertical difference.Lumbar scoliosis may show higher pelvis on the concave side due to soft tissue shortening above the iliac crest. Also screen LLD.
Leg lengthTrue and apparent leg length.True: ASIS to medial malleolus. Apparent: umbilicus to medial malleolus. Check blocks if needed.LLD can cause functional scoliosis and pelvic obliquity.
Curve flexibilityCorrection during lateral bending.Ask active side bending right and left. Compare correction or worsening with convex or concave side bending.Functional curve corrects. Structural curve remains. Flexible vertebrae correct toward convex side or worsen toward concavity. U1
Adam forward bend testTrunk asymmetry or rib hump.Patient bends forward with knees extended and arms relaxed. Measure angle of trunk rotation if scoliometer available.Screening test. Hump suggests structural rotational component.
Neurological screenStrength, sensation, reflexes, UMN signs, gait, bowel or bladder symptoms.Brief neuro exam of lower limbs and trunk if indicated.Neurological deficits are atypical in simple AIS and require more careful evaluation.
Pain and muscleLocation of pain, spasm, trigger areas, fatigue.Palpate paraspinals, QL, hip flexors, gluteals. Assess movement-related pain.Pain source may be mechanical, muscular, facet/disc related, or non-idiopathic red flag.
Respiratory functionDyspnea, chest expansion, exercise tolerance, restrictive signs.Chest expansion, breathing pattern, 6MWT or spirometry if available.Severe thoracic curves can reduce chest wall mechanics, TLC and VC, and may affect PaCO2 or oxygenation. U2
Quick bedside interpretation: convex side versus concave side
FindingConvex sideConcave sideReasoning
RibsPosterior rib humpRib valley or sunken appearanceVertebral rotation carries ribs posteriorly on convex side and anteriorly on concave side.
ScapulaMore prominentLess prominentTrunk and rib cage rotation make scapular contour asymmetric.
Breast/chest anteriorlyMay appear smaller or less prominentMay appear fuller depending on rib displacementThoracic rotation changes anterior chest contour.
Soft tissuesRelatively elongatedRelatively shortenedConcavity tends to develop capsular, ligamentous, muscular, and disc shortening.

Radiology and measurement

Cobb angle measurement

  1. Use standing spine radiograph when possible for clinical decision-making.
  2. Identify the upper and lower end vertebrae whose endplates are most tilted toward each other.
  3. Draw a line along the superior endplate of the upper end vertebra and the inferior endplate of the lower end vertebra.
  4. Measure the angle of intersection, or the angle formed by perpendiculars to these two lines.
  5. Document curve location, direction, Cobb angle, end vertebrae, apical vertebra, and comparison with previous images.

The uploaded note includes Radiopaedia-style steps for Cobb measurement. SRS also describes Cobb measurement as the standard degree measurement for scoliosis. U1 SRS

Radiological checklist

ItemHow to interpretWhy it matters
Major curveLargest primary curve.Guides diagnosis, bracing, surgical referral, and rehab focus.
Minor curveSmaller compensatory curve.May reflect balance compensation.
Structural curve on bendingIn notes, structural curve can be defined as at least 25 degrees on ipsilateral bending.Important for surgical planning and prognosis. U2
Coronal balanceDistance between C7 plumb line and CSVL. More than 2 cm is abnormal in the note.Explains trunk shift and compensatory posture. U2
Sagittal balanceDistance between plumb line and posterosuperior S1. More than 2 cm is abnormal in the note.Influences function, energy cost, and post-op posture. U2
Nash Moe rotationPedicle symmetry to grade vertebral rotation from 0 to 4.Connects radiology to rib hump and trunk rotation. U2
Risser gradeIliac apophysis ossification from lateral to medial and fusion.Estimates skeletal maturity and progression risk. U2
In-brace correction targetNote target: 30 percent for thoracic curve, 50 percent for thoracolumbar or lumbar curve.Helps evaluate brace adequacy. U2

Treatment decision algorithm

Core rule for exams: treatment is not decided by Cobb angle alone. Always combine Cobb angle with age, skeletal maturity, progression rate, symptoms, curve type, respiratory status, pain, neurological status, cosmetic and psychosocial impact, and patient or family preference. U2 SOSORT

Conservative versus surgical pathway

Confirm scoliosisStanding radiograph, Cobb angle, rotation, curve location, maturity.
Screen causeIdiopathic versus functional, congenital, neuromuscular, syndromic, painful or neurological red flag.
Mild curveUsually less than 20 to 25 degrees: observation, education, PSSE, monitor growth.
Moderate curveAround 20 to 40 or 45 degrees with growth: brace plus PSSE, adherence support, follow-up.
Severe or progressiveMore than 40 to 45 degrees, failed brace, or high progression risk: spine surgeon discussion.
Post-op rehabProtect fusion, restore ADL, progressive core and conditioning, return to sport after clearance.
Curve categoryUsual managementRehabilitation prescription focusMonitoring
Mild, usually less than 20 to 25 degreesObservation, education, scoliosis-specific exercise if appropriate.Postural education, 3D autocorrection, breathing awareness, general fitness, pain prevention.Clinical and radiographic follow-up based on growth and risk, often every 4 to 12 months in growing patients per notes.
Moderate, commonly 20 to 40 or 45 degrees with skeletal immaturityBrace or orthosis plus PSSE.Brace adherence, skin care, in-brace and out-of-brace exercise, trunk endurance, respiratory exercise, activity participation.Assess in-brace correction, adherence, growth, Cobb change, respiratory symptoms, psychosocial tolerance.
Severe, progressive, or failed conservative careSurgical referral, often posterior spinal fusion for AIS when indicated.Prehabilitation, breathing, core and lower limb conditioning, post-op protection, ADL retraining, graded walking, return to school and sport planning.Surgeon-led imaging and restrictions, functional and pain monitoring, complications surveillance.
Surgical indications from the uploaded review

The postoperative review lists common indications for surgery: curve progression despite proper brace use, curve more than 50 degrees, very progressive curve, severe scoliosis-related pain, psychological decompensation related to deformity, progressive thoracic lordosis with rib hump more than 5 cm, and significantly unbalanced curve. It also notes a broader practical threshold of more than 45 degrees in many contexts. U2

Practical rehabilitation prescriptions

Physiotherapeutic scoliosis-specific exercise (PSSE)

PSSE is not a generic back exercise program. It is curve-pattern specific, teaches active self-correction in three dimensions, integrates corrected posture into ADL, stabilizes the corrected posture, and includes patient education. The uploaded notes mention Schroth, SEAS, Side Shift, Lyon, and Dobomed as named approaches aligned with SOSORT principles. U2 SOSORT

ElementPractical prescriptionClinical cue
EducationExplain curve pattern, growth risk, goals, what brace and exercise can and cannot do.“We train control and alignment, not only muscle strength.”
3D self-correctionPractice elongation, translation, derotation, and sagittal profile correction according to curve pattern.Use mirror feedback, tactile cue, wall cue, breathing expansion toward collapsed regions.
StabilizationHold corrected posture in standing, sitting, quadruped, and functional positions.Progress from static to dynamic and from simple to task-specific.
ADL integrationApply corrected posture during sitting, schoolwork, carrying bag, gait, and sport-specific drills.Use frequent short practice blocks instead of only clinic-based sessions.
Home programDaily short practice is preferred. Duration depends on age, curve severity, attention, and adherence.Use a simple diary, caregiver involvement, and photo or mirror feedback.

Brace and orthosis prescription logic

ItemPrescription pointExam reasoning
IndicationGrowing patient, often curve above 20 degrees, especially with documented progression of 5 to 10 degrees within 6 months or moderate curve range.Brace is primarily to prevent progression during growth, not to “straighten” a mature spine.
TypeMilwaukee CTLSO, Boston TLSO, Munster scoliosis orthosis, or curve-specific TLSO depending on curve apex and orthotist/spine team plan.Higher thoracic curves may need more proximal control. Thoracolumbar and lumbar curves may be manageable with TLSO.
In-brace targetUploaded review states target correction around 30 percent for thoracic curves and 50 percent for thoracolumbar or lumbar curves.In-brace radiograph helps evaluate mechanical effectiveness. U2
Exercise with braceTeach breathing, ADL, skin care, walking, posture, and hip or shoulder mobility while respecting brace constraints.Brace adherence improves when discomfort, skin issues, and function are actively managed.
WeaningDecided by spine team based on skeletal maturity and curve stability.Do not stop brace based only on symptom relief.
Board exam caution: do not prescribe brace in isolation. Always mention education, adherence support, skin checks, psychosocial impact, and PSSE.

Respiratory and aerobic conditioning

ProblemPrescriptionProgressionMonitoring
Reduced chest wall mobilitySegmental costal breathing, thoracic expansion exercise, corrected-posture breathing.Start in supported sitting or supine, progress to standing and functional tasks.Dyspnea, chest expansion, oxygen saturation if symptomatic, fatigue.
DeconditioningWalking, cycling, swimming if appropriate, low to moderate intensity.Increase duration first, then intensity. Keep posture quality.RPE, HR, symptoms, 6MWT if available.
Severe thoracic curve or pre-op patientBreathing exercise, airway clearance if secretion problem, inspiratory muscle training if indicated by assessment.Coordinate with surgical and pulmonary team.Spirometry, VC, TLC, exercise tolerance, cough strength when relevant.

The uploaded review notes that severe scoliosis can reduce TLC and VC, may increase PaCO2, and that pulmonary rehabilitation can support surgical effectiveness in appropriate patients. U2

Pain, function, and participation

Clinical issuePrescriptionAvoid
Mechanical pain or muscle spasmEducation, activity modification, heat or cold when appropriate, gentle mobility, trunk endurance, hip flexibility, ergonomic sitting.Over-focusing on passive modalities without correcting load and movement behavior.
Poor trunk enduranceLow-load core endurance, hip and scapular control, walking or swimming, progressive functional strengthening.High-load spinal rotation or extension drills in painful or unstable phases.
Body image and adherenceValidate concerns, set functional goals, involve family, use objective progress markers.Describing treatment as purely cosmetic or minimizing distress.
Sport participationEncourage safe participation according to curve severity, symptoms, treatment phase, and surgeon restrictions if post-op.Unnecessary blanket restriction from all sport.

Postoperative scoliosis rehabilitation

Safety principle: after fusion or instrumentation, the surgeon’s protocol, fusion level, approach, implant stability, wound status, neurological status, and radiographic healing override generic timelines. The rehab prescription below is an exam guide derived from the uploaded postoperative review. U2

Why rehabilitation timing matters

Postoperative rehabilitation balances two priorities: mobilize early to prevent deconditioning and secondary morbidity, while avoiding excessive stress on healing tissue, fusion mass, and instrumentation. The uploaded review highlights osseointegration, bone graft fusion, neutral zone and elastic zone concepts, and core stabilization as the basis for progression. U2

Healing conceptTimeline or idea from notesRehab implication
OsseointegrationOsteoblast and mesenchymal migration begins around day 1; trabecular formation around 10 to 14 days; lamellar and woven bone visible by about 3 months; remodeling may continue up to 1 year.Do not overload early. Progress when the spine team confirms stability and healing.
Bone graft fusionInflammatory phase 1 to 2 weeks, proliferative phase 3 to 12 weeks, maturation and remodeling 3 to 6 months up to 1 year.Respect activity restriction in early phases. More intensive activity usually waits until later healing.
Core stabilizationMultifidus and transversus abdominis are emphasized, with internal oblique, paraspinals and pelvic floor.Train trunk control and load transfer from upper to lower body, not just isolated exercises.
Early mobilityEarly sitting, standing, walking, breathing exercise, and ankle pump are central.Prevent pulmonary, thrombotic, pain, and deconditioning complications.

Spinal protection principles

ActivityInstructionReason
Bed mobilityUse log roll. Keep trunk aligned, hips and knees flexed, avoid twisting.Protect fusion and incision while enabling independent transfer.
SittingUse a higher chair initially. Avoid prolonged sitting. Stand and walk briefly every 20 to 30 minutes when tolerated.Reduces sustained load and stiffness.
LiftingAvoid bending, lifting, and twisting early. Uploaded review uses a practical early caution of avoiding heavy lifting over about 8 pounds.Reduces stress on instrumentation and healing tissues.
Reaching floorUse squat or hip-knee strategy while keeping spine neutral.Prevents flexion and rotation stress.
Toileting and bathingConsider raised toilet. Bathing follows wound and surgeon instruction. Avoid bathtub early if not cleared.Prevents falls, wound issues, and excessive bending.
DrivingUsually after narcotic analgesics are stopped, coordination and reaction are safe, and brace restrictions allow. Surgeon clearance is needed.Safety and trunk mobility limitation.

Five phase postoperative protocol

Phase I: Preoperative preparation

Goal: educate patient and family, prepare home, teach log roll and spinal protection, optimize nutrition, conditioning, respiratory function, and expectations.

Prescription: curve-specific or general conditioning as tolerated, breathing exercise, walking, gentle flexibility, core activation, practice transfers, home setup such as bed height, toilet elevation, and avoiding stairs if possible early after discharge.

Phase II: Active rest, day 1 to week 6

Goal: inflammation and pain control, wound and complication monitoring, early mobility, safe discharge, ADL protection.

Prescription: deep breathing, ankle pumps, log roll, sitting tolerance, short walks. Notes describe walking about 5 minutes, 3 to 4 times on the first postoperative day if no special surgical warning, with gradual increase according to tolerance. Stop if sharp pain occurs.

Avoid: bending, twisting, heavy lifting, prolonged sitting, unsupported activity, aggressive spinal ROM or strengthening.

Phase III: Maximum protection, week 6 to week 12

Goal: maintain spinal protection while reintroducing controlled stabilization.

Prescription: stable trunk with upper and lower limb movements in supine, pelvic tilt in supine or standing if cleared, low intensity walking, gentle lower limb flexibility, chest wall mobility. X-ray may be obtained around 6 weeks depending on surgeon protocol.

Clinical logic: trunk should stay quiet while limbs move. Avoid excessive spinal movement.

Phase IV: Minimal protection, month 3 to month 6

Goal: activate multiple muscle groups and progress core, pelvic girdle, hip, shoulder, and aerobic conditioning.

Prescription: dynamic lumbar stabilization when radiology confirms fusion formation, walking and swimming-type whole-body exercise if cleared, hip flexor, piriformis, gluteal, and hamstring flexibility, progressive ADL and school/work conditioning.

Avoid: high-impact exercise and uncontrolled rotation unless specifically cleared.

Phase V: Dynamic phase, month 6 to 12 and beyond

Goal: dynamic stabilization, balance, gait, agility, and graded return to desired activity or sport after fusion is considered solid.

Prescription: advanced stabilization in supine, prone and standing, resistance exercise with protected technique, gait and agility drills, sport-specific preparation. Discuss high-impact and collision sports with surgeon. Recent reviews suggest many AIS patients return to sport within one year after posterior spinal fusion, but timing and performance vary with individual and surgical factors. RTS

Exercises commonly avoided or delayed after surgery

The uploaded review illustrates several exercises to avoid after scoliosis surgery, especially when they create excessive spinal flexion, rotation, axial load, or uncontrolled trunk movement. Examples include shoulder stand, sit-up, diagonal crunch, fast rotation machines, kneeling abdominal pullover, weighted abdominal machine, shoulder press, leg press, leg curl machine, vibration machines, gluteus machine, and back extension machines. U2

Clinical translation: avoid exercises where the spine becomes the moving lever or load-bearing hinge before fusion strength and surgeon clearance are established.

Exercises commonly used later when cleared

The review also lists exercises that can be useful after proper healing and clearance: pelvic tilt, straight leg raise, prone abdominal activation, plank, side plank, back strengthening, high plank mountain climber, bent arm pullover, lying down fly, seated cable row, lat pulldown, and assisted dip machine. These are not automatic early prescriptions. They should be introduced according to phase, pain, trunk control, fusion status, and surgical restrictions. U2

30 minute OSCE sequence

Efficient exam strategy: do not scatter the examination. Group by position: standing first for deformity, forward bend and balance; sitting for history and neuro screen; supine for LLD, hip and flexibility; then summarize radiology, problems, goals, and prescription.
TimeTaskMust mentionReasoning
0 to 3 minIntroduce, consent, privacy, chief complaint.Cosmetic concern, pain, dyspnea, function, activity limitation.Builds rapport and identifies the main problem.
3 to 8 minFocused history.Onset, progression, previous X-rays, brace or therapy, menarche/growth, family history, neuro symptoms, bowel/bladder, psychosocial issues.Progression and skeletal maturity determine management.
8 to 15 minStanding posterior and lateral inspection.Shoulder, scapula, rib hump, waist triangle, pelvic obliquity, trunk shift, sagittal profile, plumb line.Most visible deformity signs are standing findings.
15 to 19 minAdam forward bend and scoliometer if available.Rib hump, lumbar prominence, angle of trunk rotation, structural suspicion.Best fast screen for rotation.
19 to 23 minLeg length and curve flexibility.True and apparent LLD, active side bending correction, pelvic leveling if needed.Differentiates functional from structural contribution.
23 to 26 minNeuro, pain, respiratory quick screen.Strength, reflexes, sensation, gait if safe, chest expansion or dyspnea history.Rules out atypical or complicated scoliosis.
26 to 30 minSummarize and prescribe.Cobb angle, maturity, curve type, progression, treatment category, rehab plan, follow-up, red flags.Shows clinical reasoning and PM&R role.

One-minute oral answer template

“This patient has suspected or confirmed scoliosis, a three dimensional spinal deformity. I will classify it by curve location, Cobb angle, rotation, skeletal maturity, progression, and etiology. I will screen red flags and neurological symptoms. Management depends on severity and growth: observation and PSSE for mild curves, brace plus PSSE for moderate progressive curves in growing patients, and surgical referral for severe, progressive or failed conservative cases. Rehabilitation goals are to prevent progression, optimize posture, breathing, pain, function, participation, and after surgery to restore function without compromising fusion integrity.”

Common examiner questions

QuestionShort answer
Why is rib hump on the convex side?Vertebral rotation carries the ribs posteriorly on the convex side, producing posterior prominence.
What is the role of PSSE?Curve-specific 3D self-correction, ADL integration, stabilization, and education to reduce progression risk and improve function.
When do you use brace?Growing patient with moderate curve or documented progression, commonly around 20 to 40 or 45 degrees depending on maturity and risk.
What makes you suspect non-idiopathic scoliosis?Neurological deficit, bowel or bladder symptom, severe or night pain, fever, young age with atypical features, syndromic signs, congenital anomalies.
What is your early post-op priority?Pain control, wound and neurological monitoring, breathing, ankle pump, log roll, sitting, walking, ADL protection, avoid bending lifting twisting.

Use this to rehearse reasoning. It does not replace clinical judgment.

Quiz hub

Click an answer to check. These are designed for intermediate to advanced board exam recall.

Sources and citation map

The source tags used in this HTML are mapped below. Uploaded notes were treated as the main knowledge base. External sources were used to verify diagnostic threshold, conservative treatment principles, and current return-to-sport evidence.

  1. U1 Uploaded file: Scoliosis.docx. Contains SRS definition threshold, conservative goals, physical examination checklist, TRACE note, and Cobb angle measurement notes.
  2. U2 Uploaded file: R1 INA_ rehabilitasi pasca operasi skoliosis.pdf. Contains definition, anatomy, epidemiology, etiopathogenesis, classification, diagnosis, radiological assessment, conservative and surgical treatment, postoperative rehab phases, osseointegration, fusion, core stabilization, spinal protection, and exercise examples.
  3. IMG1 User-provided image added to this HTML: Duval-Beaupère growth and scoliosis progression curve, used as the visual anchor for puberty-related progression risk.
  4. IMG2 User-provided image added to this HTML: Cobb angle and coronal balance schematic with Radiopaedia watermark and CC BY-NC-SA label, used as the visual anchor for radiographic reporting.
  5. SRS Scoliosis Research Society. Diagnosing Scoliosis. Used for Cobb method and diagnostic threshold above 10 degrees.
  6. SOSORT Negrini S, Donzelli S, Aulisa AG, et al. 2018. 2016 SOSORT guidelines: orthopaedic and rehabilitation treatment of idiopathic scoliosis during growth. DOI: 10.1186/s13013-017-0145-8.
  7. PSSE Berdishevsky H, Lebel VA, Bettany-Saltikov J, et al. 2016. Physiotherapy scoliosis-specific exercises: state of the art review. Used through the uploaded source discussion and standard PSSE concepts.
  8. Fan Fan Y, Ren Q, To MK, Cheung JPY. 2020. Effectiveness of scoliosis-specific exercises for alleviating adolescent idiopathic scoliosis. BMC Musculoskeletal Disorders.
  9. DeLisa Frontera WR, et al. 2019. DeLisa's Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation: Principles and Practice, scoliosis and spinal deformity content cited in the uploaded review.
  10. Post-op Canbulat N, Freedman C, Ramirez J, Stevens D, and other postoperative sources listed in the uploaded review.
  11. RTS Barile F, et al. 2021 and Nassar JE, et al. 2026, return to sport after posterior spinal fusion in AIS. Used for the updated return-to-sport note.
Clinical limitation: this guide is for examination preparation and clinical reasoning. It should be adapted to local protocols, surgeon restrictions, patient diagnosis, fusion level, neurological status, cardiopulmonary status, and available orthotic resources.
